one_line_takeaway: FAMR reframes post-hoc class unlearning in vision transformers as a single anchored-regularization gradient-descent objective (uniform-KL forgetting loss + L2 anchor to original weights), driving forgotten-class accuracy to ~0% while keeping retained accuracy largely intact on CIFAR-100/ImageNet-100 ViT backbones, but the paper offers no baseline comparison against other unlearning methods and no verification of its own theoretical assumptions on the models actually tested.
key_finding: On CIFAR-100, FAMR drives forgotten-class accuracy to 1.3% (ViT-Ti), 0.9% (ViT-S), 0.0% (ViT-B), and 0.0% (ViT-L), while retaining 70.1%, 72.5%, 73.8%, and 74.2% accuracy respectively on non-forgotten classes; cross-entropy, entropy, and KL divergence on the forget set all rise monotonically with model size (e.g., KL from 2.41 for ViT-Ti to 3.10 for ViT-L). On ImageNet-100 the pattern is analogous: For-Acc of 2.1%, 1.1%, 0.0%, 0.0% for ViT-Ti/S/B/L against Ret-Acc of 76.2%, 77.4%, 79.1%, 80.3%. The paper's stated interpretation is that larger backbones (ViT-B, ViT-L) achieve more complete forgetting (For-Acc = 0.0%) together with better retained-accuracy preservation than the smaller ViT-Ti/ViT-S models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Forget-Aligned Model Reconstruction (FAMR), a post-hoc machine unlearning method for deep image classifiers. FAMR poses forgetting as a constrained optimization problem: minimize a task-specific forgetting loss on a 'forget set' T (driving predictions toward the uniform distribution via a KL-divergence term, and/or driving away from a target style via a Gram-matrix perceptual loss) plus an L2 'anchor' penalty λ/2·||θ−θ0||² that keeps the updated parameters close to the originally trained weights θ0. Optimization is done with a small number of plain gradient-descent steps (Algorithm 1), requiring no retraining from scratch and no architectural changes. The authors derive a stationarity condition (∇L_forget(θ*)+λ(θ*−θ0)=0), connect the FAMR solution to the classical influence-function first-order approximation of exact retraining (Eq. 7-9, showing ||θ*−w*||=O(λ/λ²_min(H)·Σ∇ℓ)), and give a Lipschitz-based bound relating output divergence ||fθ*(x)−fw*(x)|| to ||θ*−w*|| (Eq. 10). Appendix theorems add a linear-convergence guarantee under strong convexity/smoothness and a 'forgetting certificate' proposition stating that for any δ>0 there exists λ such that max_{x∈T}||pθ*(y|x)−u||₁ ≤ δ.
threat_model: Not applicable in the adversarial sense. This is not an attack/defense paper: there is no adversary, attacker capability, or exploit. The motivating scenario is regulatory/compliance (GDPR 'right to be forgotten'): a data owner or regulator requests that a trained model's dependence on specific training samples, an entire class, or a stylistic attribute be removed, and the goal is to satisfy that request cheaply without full retraining. The paper's own Impact Statement flags a possible misuse case (selective erasure of audit trails or uneven application of forgetting across populations) but does not develop this into a formal threat model, attacker, or evaluation.
limitations: Limitations that are evident from the text itself (the paper does not include an explicit Limitations section): (1) the empirical evaluation covers only class-level forgetting of a single randomly chosen class per run — sample-level and style-level forgetting, though formulated in Section 2, are never evaluated experimentally in the paper; (2) no comparison against any prior unlearning baseline is reported, so FAMR's numbers cannot be judged relative to SISA, influence-function retraining, or neuron-masking approaches mentioned in Related Work; (3) the theoretical guarantees (stationarity, O(λ/λ²_min(H)·Σ∇ℓ) approximation bound, Lipschitz output-divergence bound, linear convergence, forgetting certificate) all rely on strong convexity/smoothness assumptions on L_forget and access to (or bounds on) the Hessian H of the full training loss, which are not verified to hold for the deep ViT models used in the experiments; (4) there is no membership-inference-style or other empirical audit confirming that 'forgotten' information is actually unrecoverable from the model, only indirect proxies (accuracy, entropy, KL) on the forget set; (5) results are reported for a single forgotten class per configuration rather than aggregated over multiple forget-set choices, and no variance/multiple-seed statistics are given in the main tables.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-100 ViT-Ti: Ret-Acc 70.1%, For-Acc 1.3%, CE 3.42, Ent 2.21, KL 2.41
CIFAR-100 ViT-S: Ret-Acc 72.5%, For-Acc 0.9%, CE 3.55, Ent 2.33, KL 2.77
CIFAR-100 ViT-B: Ret-Acc 73.8%, For-Acc 0.0%, CE 3.91, Ent 2.43, KL 3.02
CIFAR-100 ViT-L: Ret-Acc 74.2%, For-Acc 0.0%, CE 4.02, Ent 2.49, KL 3.10
ImageNet-100 ViT-Ti: Ret-Acc 76.2%, For-Acc 2.1%, CE 3.14, Ent 2.28, KL 2.65
ImageNet-100 ViT-S: Ret-Acc 77.4%, For-Acc 1.1%, CE 3.49, Ent 2.45, KL 2.93
ImageNet-100 ViT-B: Ret-Acc 79.1%, For-Acc 0.0%, CE 3.74, Ent 2.59, KL 3.11
ImageNet-100 ViT-L: Ret-Acc 80.3%, For-Acc 0.0%, CE 3.88, Ent 2.63, KL 3.17
